title: François Villeroy de Galhau: Navigating an uncertain world - steering monetary policy in the right direction to hold our course
summary: Bank of France Governor François Villeroy de Galhau delivered an introductory portion of a speech at Sciences Po on April 2, 2026, discussing whether recent geopolitical events (war in the Middle East) might replicate the 2022 energy price shock. The available excerpt describes the start of 2026 monetary policy as being in a favorable "2+2" configuration (2% inflation, 2% interest rate). The Governor begins to address why we are not witnessing a repeat of 2022. Note: this is only the introductory portion (~398 words) of a 15-page speech; substantive policy arguments are not included in this excerpt.
key_points: The speech was delivered by François Villeroy de Galhau, Governor of the Bank of France, at Sciences Po, Paris on April 2, 2026
Villeroy describes the start of 2026 monetary policy as being in a favorable "2+2" position: 2% inflation and 2% interest rate
The speech addresses whether the war in the Middle East might cause a repeat of the 2022 energy price shock scenario
Villeroy states the answer at this stage is "no" - we are not witnessing a repeat of 2022
The oil price shock is described as significant, particularly given lower gas prices before the February 2026 shock
The speech references a 15-page PDF containing the full text (696kb) which is not included in this excerpt
why_it_matters: As a G7 central bank governor, Villeroy's assessment of whether current geopolitical developments will replicate 2022's inflationary shock could be significant for market expectations around ECB policy trajectory. However, the available excerpt only contains introductory framing; the substantive arguments and policy conclusions are not accessible in this truncated version. This record should be updated once the full 15-page PDF is retrieved.
macro_context: The available excerpt directly addresses macro catalyst risks - specifically whether an energy price shock from Middle East conflict could reignite inflation similar to 2022. Villeroy begins to argue 2022 is not repeating, but the full argument is not included. The '2+2' framing describes a favorable starting position at the beginning of 2026.
